observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a) The City Mayor submit documents or proof that the City has generated monetary savings as a result of the suggestions, inventions, superior accomplishments and other personal efforts of the employees or group of employees as basis for the grant of monetary awards using the SACAPRAISE as authority. b) The San Carlos City PRAISE Committee submit evaluation reports or any documentation recognizing the productivity, creativeness made or ethical behavior of employees or group of employees making them eligible for the PRAISE monetary awards and incentives. c) The City accountant ensure that the amount of incentives to be granted shall not exceed 20% of the monetary savings generated from inventions, suggestions and superior accomplishments pursuant to the policies of the CSC approved San Carlos City PRAISE. d) The City submit specific legal basis for the grant of Achievement Incentives in the event that it could not submit aforementioned requirements. observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to restate the financial statements by effecting the following proposed adjusting journal entries in the books:

We also recommended that Management require the City Engineer to provide a status/progress report on completed and on-going infrastructure projects on a quarterly basis to the City Accountant as basis in recording the appropriate PPE and liability accounts.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements information about the loss of property due to fire, the details of the loss, the events causing the loss, the reason that no accounting journal entries have been made for such loss and any subsequent loss of property that may be due to calamity or similar fortuitous events to ensure that relevant information are available to the intended users of the financial statements.

4.6 	We recommended that Management require the City Budget Officer to strictly monitor and control the appropriations, allotments and obligations through the preparation and maintenance of RAAOs following the prescribed format and guidelines to prevent incurrence of expenditure with no or insufficient appropriation and/or charging to incorrect budgetary item in the approved and supplemental budgets.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC to refrain from recommending the award of contracts to bidders whose registered line of business does not align with the goods to be procured.
5.8 	We also recommended that Management instruct the BAC and Technical Working Group (TWG) to conduct a more thorough post-qualification process by carefully reviewing the relevant permits and licenses required for the goods or services to be delivered or performed.

7.8 	We recommended that Management:
Require the City Engineer to submit the Results of Test Analysis Report on the completed projects for technical evaluation and inspection by the COA Regional Technical Audit Group and ensure that contractors perform material testing with DOST/BRS accredited laboratories and submit the results to the Accounting Office for payment processing; and
Direct the City Accountant to ensure that Results of Test Analysis Report from DOST/BRS accredited laboratories for all infrastructure procurement are secured before processing of payment.

We recommended that Management require the BAC, in coordination with the GSO, to stop the practice of tailor-fitting the technical specifications to a specific brand and ensure that all specifications are generic to encourage competition and obtain the most advantageous price for the government.

We recommended that Management direct the BAC members to ensure that procurements involving goods of similar specifications or quality are consolidated into a single requisition to secure more favorable pricing and terms and promote efficiency in the procurement process.

We recommended that Management instruct the GAD Focal Person to:
Incorporate PPAs in the Annual GAD Plan and Budget that are responsive to the gender issues of the city government constituents;
Stop the inclusion of the allocation for cash subsidy for solo parents in the GAD Plan and Budget, as well as PPAs that do not address gender-related issues and concerns; and
Closely monitor the optimum implementation of GAD PPAs to ensure that they are effective, results-oriented, and genuinely responsive to identified gender-related issues.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant, in coordination with the BAC Secretariat, to:
Submit all the required documents to support the propriety and validity of payments of various transactions and ensure the completeness of documentary requirements before processing of payment for future transactions;
Ensure that a certification from the Accountant of the DepEd SDO San Carlos City is secured prior to payment of succeeding financial transactions chargeable to SEF; and
Establish and implement control mechanisms to ensure the consistent submission of complete and proper supporting documents.

We recommended that Management direct the City Agriculturist to:
Formulate clear and concrete guidelines, including qualification criteria, for identifying beneficiaries to ensure equitable access to income-generating opportunities, particularly among economically disadvantaged fisherfolks which should be submitted to the Head of the Agency for approval; and
Draft a MOA between the City and the beneficiaries, to be approved by the Head of the Procuring Entity.  The MOA should outline the respective responsibilities of both parties and provide a framework for effective monitoring and compliance.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Solid Waste Management Board, in coordination with the CENRO, to evaluate, prepare, and propose revenue generation measures from the fertilizers to the Sangguniang Panlungsod.
We also recommended that Management assign additional manpower in the CENRO who will operate and utilize both the mobile composter and the manual composter to enhance the efficiency, effectiveness, and economy of public fund utilization.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to: a) Deposit the daily collections intact to the authorized depository bank within the day or not later than the next banking day, and to avoid having huge amounts of undeposited collections, in accordance with Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992; and b) Instruct the collectors/tellers and BTs to turn over all of their collections intact to the cashier at the close of the business day on the last working day of every year, in compliance with Item 26 of aforementioned Circular.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:

Regularly assess at each reporting date whether there is any indication that an asset may be impaired and estimate its recoverable amount, if any indication of impairment exists; and

Compute the impairment loss of the assets that qualify for impairment as provided by IPSAS 21 for non-cash generating assets and IPSAS 26 for cash generating assets, whichever is applicable.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:

Review the PPE account to ensure that items included therein comply with the provisions of Paragraphs 5.6 to 5.8 of the Conceptual Framework for GPFR by Public Sector Entities of the IPSAS; and

Comply with the Commission on Audit Circular No. 2020-006 dated                      January 31, 2020, which prescribes the necessary documents and processes for a one-time cleansing of the PPE account which should include the items with unreconciled balances to ensure that the account is fairly presented in the FS of the City.

We recommended and the Management agreed that:

The Acting Chief of San Carlos City Hospital coordinate with the DOH in order to expedite the release of the Deed of Donation, and forward a copy thereof and other pertinent documents of the HFEP donation to the City General Services Office;

The City General Services Office, in turn, furnish the City Accountant with the detailed list of donated medical equipment, supported by proper and complete documentation; and

The City Accountant recognize in the books the donated medical equipment received by San Carlos City Hospital including the corresponding depreciation thereon, in accordance with Section V.B.5 of DOH Administrative Order No. 2019-0048 dated October 29, 2019.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor:

Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct and complete the physical inventory of all the City’s Property, Plant, and Equipment;

Direct the Inventory Committee to undertake all planned activities/procedures in the approved PIP and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) following COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and 

Require the City General Services Department to update its property records based on the result of inventory and reconcile with accounting records to come up with the reconciled balances of PPE accounts according to Item 5.12 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated                  January 31, 2020.

Create an Inventory Committee in consonance with the composition provided under Item 5.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and require them to complete all the preliminary activities and prepare the Physical Inventory Plan before the conduct of inventory taking.

We recommended and the City Mayor and OCPDC agreed to:

Ensure that the development projects funded out of the 20 percent DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready pursuant to Item 3.2.3 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020, to be able to address the City’s constantly evolving development needs and attain its desired socio-economic targets and outcomes; and

Prioritize the implementation of projects funded out of the 20 percent DF so that the expected services and benefits are delivered to the constituents of the City without delay.

We recommended and the City Mayor, City Development Council and the Office of the City Planning and Development Coordinator agreed to:

Prepare a comprehensive plan by prioritizing the use of the lots for developmental projects that will benefit most of the constituents or promote the general welfare of the people; and

Prioritize the implementation of development projects with a definite purpose and period for implementation according to Item 3.2.3 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020, so the City could address its constantly evolving development needs and attain its desired socio-economic targets and outcomes.

We recommended that the City Mayor inform the concerned health workers that payment of Active Hazard Duty Pay for the period December 20, 2020 to   April 30, 2021 was not covered by an existing guideline and require them to return the amount received.

We recommended that:

The City Accountant ascertained the collectability of the dormant unliquidated cash advances. In case the collectability is uncertain, follow the specific guidelines in the reconciling and cleaning of accounts and prepare the quarterly aging of dormant cash advances to support the request for write-off; and

The City Mayor filed the request for write-off of dormant cash advances to the Audit Team Leader/and or Supervising Auditor pursuant to Item 8.2 COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

Instruct the City Engineer to expedite the completion of the titling of lots for the five projects so distribution of lot titles to the beneficiaries could be undertaken; and

Direct the City Assessor to include in the assessment rolls the individual subdivision lots once they are titled, assess the values of the properties and impose the appropriate taxes.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Project Engineer to address the problems encountered for non-completion of titling of lots, segregation and subdivision survey and approval of subdivision plan so that lot ownership could be established, and beneficiaries of the Homelot Program could apply for individual land title.

We recommended that the City Engineering Office:

Prepare a comprehensive plan to maximize the service potential of the amphibious excavator so the City could attain the primary purpose for purchasing said equipment to be used in dredging rivers and creeks to avoid adverse impacts of flooding; and

We recommend that the City Mayor:

Direct the City Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count of the City’s properties and submit a complete Report on the Physical Count of PPEs (RPCPPE) and Biological Assets pursuant to Section 124, of the MNGAS for LGUs Volume I; and

The City Accountant and the GSO undertake the immediate reconciliation of their records in order to establish the validity and propriety of the reported balances of PPEs and Biological Assets in the financial statements.

We recommended that:

The City Mayor submit documents or proof that the City has generated monetary savings as a result of the suggestions, inventions, superior accomplishments and other personal efforts of the employees or group of employees as basis for the grant of monetary awards using the SACAPRAISE as authority.

The San Carlos City PRAISE Committee submit evaluation reports or any documentation recognizing the productivity, creativeness made or ethical behavior of employees or group of employees making them eligible for the PRAISE monetary awards and incentives.

The City accountant ensure that the amount of incentives to be granted shall not exceed 20% of the monetary savings generated from inventions, suggestions and superior accomplishments pursuant to the policies of the CSC approved San Carlos City PRAISE.

The City submit specific legal basis for the grant of Achievement Incentives in the event that it could not submit aforementioned requirements.  Otherwise, we recommended that the said incentives be refunded by the City government officials and employees for lack of specific legal authority.

Direct the City Treasurer to immediately remit the unutilized balance of ₱100,000.00 to the BTr and furnish the City Accountant of the copy of official receipt or deposit slip for proper recording in the books of accounts.

We recommended that Management, through the Local School Board, allocate amount for MOOE in the SEF budget for the payment of utility workers hired through COS for proper recording of the corresponding expense in the books.  We, likewise, recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to check the applicable account title for proper presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to recoup the advance payment from the contractor by deducting 15 percent from his periodic progress payments.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the City Accountant to check the completeness of the documents, which includes verifying the signature of the participants on the attendance sheet and to compare the budgeted/targeted number of participants with the actual attendees to determine whether the quantity of procured meals and snacks matches with the targeted number;
Instruct the GAD Focal Person to refrain from ordering excessive quantity of foods/meals that will eventually exceed the actual number of participants and to conduct confirmation of attendees based on the targeted/budgeted number of participants prior to the procurement of meals and snacks; and
Exercise due prudence in using government funds or property allocated for GAD programs and activities.

We recommended that Management instruct the GAD Focal Person to incorporate PPAs in the Annual GAD Plan and Budget that are responsive to gender issues of the local government constituents and strictly monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs.
We also recommended that Management fully utilize the annual GAD fund in implementing GAD-related programs and projects.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the City Accountant to attach valid contracts of JO workers and ensure that attached supporting documents are original and properly signed;
Instruct the immediate supervisor to require each JO worker to indicate his actual accomplishments for a particular period in the accomplishment report duly certified correct by the JO worker and approved by the supervisor; and
Direct all JO workers to use biometrics to record daily attendance alongside a logbook duly supervised by the authorized representative of the City HRMO.

We recommended that Management direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to incorporate the Special Trust Fund for LDRRMF from previous years into the LDRRMFIP in the succeeding years.

We recommended that Management require the AO to refund the purchase price of one-unit Macbook laptop in the amount of ₱79,000.00 considering that it has been idle for several months and was not used for its intended purpose.  Thereafter, furnish the Audit Team the Official Receipt as evidence for the refund.

We recommended that Management formulate and adopt a sound system of internal controls on equipment issued to officials/employees of attached NGAs through issuance of clearance from money and property accountability by the LGU.  We likewise recommended that Management create a team or IT department focused mainly on addressing IT-related issues of various offices of the LGU.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the City HRMO to coordinate with the GSIS to update the employees’ records reflecting the updated amount of employees’ contributions to be remitted to the agency;
Require all employees with no Pag-IBIG number to immediately obtain one to maximize the benefits they can derive from the agency; and
Instruct the City Accountant to reconcile any unremitted balances to ensure the employees’ benefits from the respective agencies are not affected.

1.7	We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the:
General Services Department (GSD) and Office of the City Accountant (OCA) carry out the required reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with the property and accounting records, as outlined in Item 6.3 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
OCA to take up the necessary accounting entries to write-off/drop from the books of accounts, items of PPE tagged as unreconciled balances as prescribed in Item 6.3.2.e of the same Circular; and
GSD, OCA and other relevant departments to enhance their communication and collaboration to streamline reconciliation efforts and address and resolve discrepancies effectively.

2.7 	We recommended and the City Treasurer agreed to:
Deposit the daily collections intact to the authorized depository bank within the day or not later than the next banking day, and to avoid having huge amounts of undeposited collections, in accordance with Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992; and
Instruct the collectors/tellers and BTs to turn over all of their collections intact to the cashier at the close of the business day on the last working day of every year, in compliance with Item 26 of aforementioned Circular.

3.8 	We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Establish a technical team to facilitate assessments of the City’s assets which could potentially be subject to impairment;
Regularly assess, at each reporting date, whether there is any indication that an asset may be impaired, and estimate its recoverable amount, if any indication of impairment exists; and
Compute the impairment loss of the assets that qualify for impairment as provided by IPSAS 21 for non-cash generating assets and IPSAS 26 for cash generating assets, where applicable.

4.5	We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the:
General Services Department (GSD) and Office of the City Accountant (OCA) carry out the required reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with the property and accounting records, as outlined in Item 6.3 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020;
OCA to take up the necessary accounting entries to write-off/drop from the books of accounts, items of PPE tagged as unreconciled balances as prescribed in Item 6.3.2.e of the same Circular; and
GSD, OCA and other relevant departments to enhance their communication and collaboration to streamline reconciliation efforts and address and resolve discrepancies effectively.

5.11	We recommended and the City Mayor and CPDC agreed to:
Ensure that the development projects funded out of the 20 percent DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready, pursuant to Item 3.2.3 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1 dated November 4, 2020, to be able to address the City’s constantly evolving development needs and attain its desired socio-economic targets and outcomes;
Prioritize the implementation of projects funded out of the 20 percent DF so that the expected services and benefits are delivered to the constituents of the City without delay; and
Follow through with the planned initiatives of the City of expediting the implementation of PPAs funded out of the 20 percent DF, so as to enhance the utilization thereof.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Incorporate in the City’s LDRRMFIP the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years as prescribed in Item 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, following the format in Annex A of the Circular;
Prepare the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on a monthly basis, following the format in Annex B of the aforesaid Circular, and submit the same to the COA Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month as mandated in Item 5.1.5 of the same Circular; and
Furnish the OCD and the DILG-LGOO, copies of the annual LDRRMFIP and the monthly Reports on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, as stated in Item 5.1.6 of the Circular.

6B.6	We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Incorporate in the City’s LDRRMFIP the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years as prescribed in Item 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, following the format in Annex A of the Circular;
Prepare the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on a monthly basis, following the format in Annex B of the aforesaid Circular, and submit the same to the COA Auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month as mandated in Item 5.1.5 of the same Circular; and
Furnish the OCD and the DILG-LGOO, copies of the annual LDRRMFIP and the monthly Reports on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, as stated in Item 5.1.6 of the Circular.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the CGSO agreed to:

Promptly submit the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption and all recovered documents pertaining to fuel utilization to the City Accountant so that adjusting journal entries could be prepared to reflect the correct fuel consumptions of vehicles in the City’s books of accounts; and

Comply faithfully with the provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977 to ensure effective control on fuel, gasoline, and oil expenditures are properly accounted to avoid wasteful, excessive, and unnecessary spending on fuel.

We recommended and the City Mayor and the CDRRMO agreed to:

Expedite the implementation of the planned disaster preparedness and mitigation PPAs incorporated in the City’s LDRRMFIP in accordance with the timelines to attain a disaster-resilient community and strengthen the capacity of the organization or communities to manage and reduce disaster risks; and

Conduct an effective planning and identification of disaster preparedness and mitigation PPAs that could address the needs of the people and must be implemented within the budget year without delay before finalizing and submitting the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:

Instruct the concerned Implementing Offices in-charge of BuB funded projects to resolve the issues and constraints on late completion of the projects, coordinate with other offices for assistance and expedite the implementation of projects;

Direct the CPDCO to monitor the implementation of the BuB projects and assist the Implementing Offices in case problems in the project execution arise; and

Require the Accounting Office to update and reconcile its records with the CPDO reports regarding receipt and utilization of BuB funds including information on the physical status of the projects.

We recommended and the City Social Welfare and Development Officer agreed to:

Conduct regular monitoring and inspections of all the welfare goods received for distribution to victims of typhoon or other related disaster, identify items that are near expiry time and prioritize the distribution to deserving beneficiaries and ensure that the distribution of relief goods must be done after the natural or man-made disasters to maximize the benefits and prevent wastage of donated goods, as prescribed under Section 17.b.3.v of RA No. 7160;

Practice the First-In, First-Out (FIFO) method in releasing perishable items to warrant the distribution of donated goods before their expiration date; and

Properly store the welfare goods to guarantee that they remain in good condition and are not damaged, spoiled, or contaminated, by:

Providing appropriate storage facilities, e.g. warehouse or storage rooms, with suitable temperature, humidity, and ventilation conditions; and

Following proper storage methods and techniques, e.g. properly organizing the items and using appropriate packaging materials.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:

Direct all implementing offices to give an updated and complete report on BuB-funded projects, both ongoing and completed, to the CPDO;

Direct the CPDCO Head to immediately update the status report on the implementation of BuB-funded projects, and reconcile the disbursements and balances with the Office of the City Accountant; and

Direct the City Accountant to immediately return to the source agencies unutilized balances of BuB completed projects or revert to the general fund the excess city’s counterpart fund in adherence to COA Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended and the City Engineer agreed to direct the Engineer-In-Charge of the project to closely coordinate with the Barangay Bagonbon council to ensure timely completion of the road opening and expedite the construction of the school building at Paniagan Elementary School once the road opening is completed.

We recommended that the City Mayor, City Hospital Acting Chief and other concerned parties:

The Acting Chief of San Carlos City Hospital strengthen controls in the conduct of inspection and acceptance of donated HFEP equipment in order to properly identify those that are needed by the hospital in accordance with the focus of the program;

The Acting Chief of San Carlos City Hospital follow-up with the DOH on their offer to conduct the necessary training for hospital personnel regarding the proper use and maintenance of the donated equipment, as well as include in the hospital budget an allocation for the purchase of the reagents needed in the utilization of the medical equipment; and

The City Mayor direct the Acting Chief of Hospital, City Accountant, and CGSO to establish procedures regarding receipt of donated equipment to the City Hospital so that all offices concerned are aware of their responsibilities regarding receipt, utilization, and recording of any donations received.

We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to:

Direct the City General Services Officer to initiate immediate disposal of identified unserviceable property following Item V of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, so the City can be relieved of these properties; and

Instruct all Department/Office Heads to conduct regular evaluation on the conditions of the properties under their custody and immediately return the unserviceable items to the CGSD so it can file an application for disposal with the COA in accordance with Section 167, Rule 22 of the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the Local Governments.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

Direct the GSO to issue Property Transfer Report for the transfer of these firearms and motor vehicles to the PNP and BJMP pursuant to Item 7.1 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

Exhaust all means to collect the outstanding loans receivable from the delinquent cooperatives and people’s organizations so that this loan program will continue to have available operational funds to extend loans to other farmers and fisherfolks cooperatives; and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
Create an Inventory Committee in consonance with the composition provided under Item 5.2 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and require them to complete all the preliminary activities and prepare the Physical Inventory Plan before the conduct of inventory taking;
Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct and complete the physical inventory of all of the City’s properties and undertake all planned activities/procedures in the approved Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) and prepare the RPCPPE pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and
Require the GSO to update its property records based on the result of inventory and reconcile these with accounting records to come up with reliable balances of PPE accounts pursuant to Item 5.12 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Direct the GSO to issue Property Transfer Report for the transfer of these firearms and motor vehicles to the PNP and BJMP pursuant to Item 7.1 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and
Recommend to the Sangguniang Panlungsod, through an SP Resolution, to donate said properties to the PNP and BJMP.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the respective heads of offices of employees who were granted the AHDP and SRA to submit documents/proof that the nature of services and areas of assignments of the concerned payees during the period September 15, 2020 to April 30, 2021 were directly related to health care response or in contact with COVID-19 patients in order that they could qualify for the grants pursuant to Administrative Order Nos. 35 and 36 and DBM-DOH Joint Circular Nos. 1 and 2.

We recommended that the City Mayor inform the concerned health workers that payment of Active Hazard Duty Pay for the period December 20, 2020 to   April 30, 2021 was not covered by an existing guideline and require them to return the amount received.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Direct the respective department heads accountable for the receipt of in-kind donations to prepare and submit a one-time report for in-kind donations to NDRRMC, through the OCD, copy furnished the respective COA Auditors within ten (10) working days after the end of the quarantine period or within ten (10) working days after the end of each quarter pursuant to Item II.1.b.1 of COA Circular No. 2020-009 dated April 21, 2020; and
Require the City Department Heads to report to the City Accounting Office the inventory of undistributed donations in-kind or the remaining balances of donations for costing and recording in the books.

We recommended that:
The National Immunization Program Coordinator of the City Health Office provide the City Accountant with the wall-to-wall inventory reports, attach all Requisition Issue Slips, of all donations in-kind received by the City related to its COVID-19 Vaccination Program;
The City Accountant record in the books all donations in-kind with available cost information in accordance with Paragraph 96 of IPSAS 23; and
The City Accountant properly recognize all donations in-kind without available cost information at their fair value as at the date of receipt, which may be ascertained by reference to an active market, or by appraisal in accordance with Paragraph 97 of IPSAS 23.

We recommended that:
The City Accountant ascertain the collectability of the dormant unliquidated cash advances. In case the collectability is uncertain, follow the specific guidelines in the reconciling and cleaning of accounts and prepare the quarterly aging of dormant cash advances to support the request for write-off.
The City Mayor file the request for write-off of dormant cash advances to the Audit Team Leader/and or Supervising Auditor pursuant to Item 8.2 COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Head of the CLDO and the Accounting Office to:
Encourage delinquent cooperatives to request for restructuring of loans so the term of payment may be extended and the revised payment scheme should be viable for the cooperative members;
Exhaust all means to collect the outstanding loans receivable from the delinquent cooperatives and people’s organizations so that this loan program will continue to have available operational funds to extend loans to other farmers and fisherfolks cooperatives; and
Regularly monitor and analyze the receivable accounts of the City to ensure that these are collected when they become due and demandable pursuant to Item 6.1 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 or if after all options still fail, request for the write-off of the accounts in case the collectability could not be ascertained.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
Compute the amortization of the intangible assets not being amortized since they are being used in the operations to fairly present the Intangible Assets account in the Financial Statements and record the entries in the journal entry voucher as shown below:
Amortization Expense		₱xx
Accumulated Amortization		₱xx
Review the residual values of the fully amortized intangible assets and recognize additional amortization for these items to reduce their values to zero in compliance with the provisions of Paragraphs 99 and 100 of IPSAS 31 or disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements any deviation from the accounting standards; and
Request from the Commission on Audit (COA) for the updating of the eNGAS regarding provision on the residual value of the intangible assets to conform with Paragraphs 99 and 100 of IPSAS 31.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Instruct the City Engineer to expedite the completion of the titling of lots for the five projects so distribution of lot titles to the beneficiaries could be undertaken; and
Direct the City Assessor to include in the assessment rolls the individual subdivision lots once they are titled, assess the values of the properties and impose the appropriate taxes.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Chief of the City Hospital to:
Manage health care and other hospital wastes during or after the pandemic following the requirements in Item 5.3 of the Health Care Waste Manual for general and COVID-19 waste;
Follow the procedures for the segregation and storage of infectious wastes in accordance with Items 5.1 & 5.2 of Chapter 5 of the same Manual; and
Expedite the processing of the HWMS Generator ID of the City Hospital so that the disposal of the infectious wastes could be started to avoid overcrowding of hospital waste in the designated holding area.
We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the Head of CEMO to expedite the construction of additional vaults at the sanitary landfill to cater the disposal of health care waste.

We recommended that the City Health Office continue to exhaust extra effort to reach more individuals and convince them to get vaccinated not only to achieve the target herd immunity but also to inoculate majority of the entire population for their safety and protection against COVID-19.

We recommended that the City Engineering Office:
Prepare a comprehensive plan to maximize the service potential of the amphibious excavator so the City could attain the primary purpose for purchasing said equipment to be used in dredging rivers and creeks to avoid adverse impacts of flooding; and
Conduct regular maintenance on the equipment to regulate its wear and tear and reduce the operational limitations, enhance its performance and reliability.

contractor to submit CSHP duly approved by the DOLE to ensure
protection and welfare of the workers at the construction site and in
compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Standards set by the
DOLE.

11.5. We recommended that Management instruct the BAC to strictly comply
with the requirements and procurement procedure of 2016 Revised IRR of
RA No. 9184 in order to attain government policy of competition,
transparency, economy and efficiency in the procurement process.

12.7. We recommended that Management instruct the BAC and the GSO to
strictly adhere to the requirements and procurement procedures set forth
in the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 in order to attain the government
policy of competition, transparency, economy and efficiency in the
procurement process.

13.11. We recommended that Management:
13.11.1. Instruct the City Administrator to observe the provisions of the
2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 in hiring the services of
consultants;
13.11.2. Instruct the City Accountant and City Treasurer to observe the
processing of payments of honoraria taking into account that the
services are already rendered/performed; and
13.11.3. Instruct the City Treasurer to pay in accordance with the
payment schedule stipulated in the Contract of Service and cause
the refund of the additional payment of honoraria given to five
consultants in the total amount of ₱91,000.

14.9. We recommended that Management:
14.9.1. Require the City Accountant to refrain from charging expenses to
the GAD Fund that do not particularly address gender-related
issues and strictly adhere to the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-
NEDA JMC; and
14.9.2. Instruct the GAD Focal Point Person to strictly observe the
provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC to ensure that the
GAD funds are used on projects, programs and activities that
address gender-issues and uphold gender equality and women’s
empowerment.

15.4. We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to
certify only the loan applications of employees whose salaries could
accommodate the monthly loan amortization and would still have a net
take home pay of not less than the amount specified in the General
Provisions of GAA of each year.

16.8. We recommended that Management:
16.8.1. Instruct the City Human Resources Management Officer to
strictly adhere to the guidelines on the monetization of leave
credits provided by CSC; and
16.8.2. Instruct the City Accountant to check the completeness of the
documents for payment of terminal leave benefits and require the
submission of the lacking documents to preclude the issuance of
Notice of Suspension.

17.10. We recommended that Management:
17.10.1. Instruct the City Treasurer and the BPL Officer to adhere
strictly with the provisions of BLGF MC and City Tax Ordinance to
provide guidance on the assessment in the imposition of local
taxes and fees in the local governments during the registration and
renewal of business permits and licenses; and
17.10.2. Direct the City Treasurer to:
a. Tag all the afore-cited suppliers and be subject of
examination of books of accounts to ensure the correctness
of taxes, fees and charges to be collected; and
b. Require the suppliers to pay the underpayment of business
taxes, fees and charges in the total amount of ₱914,600.05
and require Resuello Pharmacy with unrenewed business
permit to pay the renewal fees and business taxes including
penalties and interests computed by the City Treasury
Office for the years identified and impose possible sanctions
for non-compliance with the Revenue Code.

18.7. We recommended that Management instruct the City Administrator to
observe the proper implementation with regard to the program extended
to the PUIs, PUMs and LSIs and require the CDRRMO to provide the
master list of names of PUIs, PUMs and LSIs for monitoring purposes.

19.6. We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendations that Management:
19.6.1. Instruct the Appraisal Committee in coordination with the GSIS
Insurance Division to cause the appraisal of all insurable
properties of the City;

the same to the GSIS as the basis for the assessment of general
insurance coverage of all insurable assets of the City using the
format prescribed in COA Circular No. 2018 - 002;
19.6.3. Provide the sufficient amount in the Annual Budget to pay for
the premiums of all insurable assets; and
19.6.4. Secure the property insurance of non-insured government
properties of the City.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
Direct the City Inventory Committee to conduct annual physical count of the City’s properties and submit a complete Report on the Physical Count of PPEs (RPCPPE) and Biological Assets pursuant to Section 124, of the MNGAS for LGUs Volume I; and
The City Accountant and the GSO undertake the immediate reconciliation of their records in order to establish the validity and propriety of the reported balances of PPEs and Biological Assets in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Accountant record the financial assistance granted to fisher folk associations as Due from NGOs/POs pursuant to the Revised Chart of Accounts and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries.

We recommended that:
The City Mayor, thru the CEMO, follow up the submission of the monthly progression report of the fisherfolk association and determine the status of implementation of the project; and
The City include in the MOA provisions/obligations requiring the fisherfolk associations to submit liquidation reports on succeeding assistance released to associations charged against the grant from NGAs (BFAR) so that the City could ascertain that the fund was utilized for its intended purpose.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC to follow the rules and regulations on procurement as provided under RA No. 9184 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations, and the GPPB to obtain the most advantageous price for the government.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC to follow the rules and regulations on procurement as provided under RA No. 9184 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR), and any relevant policies issued by the GPPB to obtain the most advantageous price for the government.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Stop the grant of cash advance to the LDRRMO Head to pay for the       COVID-19 related expenses of the City and require him to liquidate the amount at once; and
Direct the City Treasurer to pay the disbursements related to COVID-19 expenses thru check pursuant to the General Principle No. 2 of the Imprest System under COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the BAC to require the supplier to submit an Omnibus Sworn Statement pursuant to the requirement of RA No. 9184 and its RIRR and the GPPB.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Direct the BAC to update its Annual Procurement Plan (APP), prepare the Procurement Management Reports (PMRs), post to the City’s official website and submit to the GPPB the foregoing reports in consonance with GPPB Circular 02-2020 dated May 20, 2020 in order to promote transparency and accountability in the procurement activities; and
Direct the end user or implementing office and BAC to undertake changes in the individual PPMPs and consolidated APP every six months or as often as may be required by the HoPE and submit the same to the GPPB within the prescribed period set forth by the latter.

We recommended that the City Mayor to reassess the type and model of mobile phones to be issued to Sangguniang Panlungsod Members taking into consideration economy and efficiency in the use of government funds.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Augment the appropriation for insurance premiums for large farm animals so that all carabaos and cattle distributed to recipient beneficiaries are insured in compliance with the provisions of the Memorandum of Agreement and to protect the City from any unexpected loss due to death, disease or other risks; and
Direct the personnel assigned in the Carabao and Cattle Dispersal Program of the Office of the City Agriculture to thoroughly review the listing of large farm animals to be insured and ascertain that only living livestock are included in the insurance coverage.

We recommended that the City Mayor promptly inform the contractor of the deficiencies observed on the project and demand for immediate repair of these deficiencies at the expense of the contractor pursuant to Section 62, Rule XIX of     RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Project Engineer to address the problems encountered for non-completion of titling of lots, segregation and subdivision survey and approval of subdivision plan so that lot ownership could be established and beneficiaries of the Homelot Program could apply for individual land title.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to record the purchase of supplies and materials in appropriate Inventory account and timely reflect as Expense the distribution or issuance of the supplies and materials to end-users.
We also recommended that Management direct the City General Services Officer to provide Supplies and Materials Consumption Report and Report on the Physical Count of Inventories of unissued items to the City Accountant to serve as the basis of recording adjustments to the Inventories account.
We likewise recommended that Management require the City Accountant and General Services Officer to maintain supply ledger cards and property cards, respectively, for each item of the Inventory account.
We further recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to prepare the following adjusting entries to establish the balance of undistributed inventories to rectify the effects in the government equity account:
Accounts | Debit | Credit
Property and Equipment for Distribution | 1,563,000.00 | 
Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies Inventory | 633,075.00 | 
Other Supplies and Materials Inventory | 3,397,912.00 | 
Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies Expense |  | 3,733,355.00
Other Supplies and Materials Expense |  | 1,860,632.00

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entry in order to correct the affected account balances.
The suggested journal entry is as follows:
Account | Debit | Credit
Other Property, Plant and Equipment | 2,500,000.00 | 
Accounts Payable |  | 2,500,000.00
Account | Debit | Credit
Accounts Payable | 2,500,000.00 | 
Other Property, Plant and Equipment |  | 2,500,000.00

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to reconcile its records of outstanding loans payable with the bank records and effect the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balance of Loans Payable account in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to locate the documents or records to identify the complete details of the Loans Receivable - Others account in the amount of ₱4,341,306.33 and to update the accounting records.
We also recommended that Management direct the City Cooperative Officer to issue final demand letters to the borrowers and enforce payment in order to collect the receivables from the borrowers to provide funds for other intended priority projects of the City. In case collections thereof could no longer be ascertained, the Management through the City Accountant may request authority to write-off the dormant receivable account to clean the account subject to the requirements and procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the City Accountant to make a correcting entry to reclassify the cash advances granted to City officials and employees as Advances to Officers and Employees account in the amount of ₱1,134,306.79 considering that these cash advances pertained to travelling and operating expenses;
Require the City Accountant to send demand letters to officials and employees requiring them to liquidate/settle their cash advances immediately since the purpose for which these were given has been served; and
Require the City Treasurer to deduct/withhold the salaries of the City officials and employees their unliquidated cash advances considering that they have been notified and given more adequate time to liquidate their cash advances.

We recommended that Management instruct the City General Services Officer and Head of the Appraisal Committee to facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable properties to prevent further deterioration and loss of value and generate additional funds from the sale thereof.

We recommended that Management instruct the City General Services Officer, in cooperation with the City Agriculturist, to submit an Inventory of Breeding Stocks including report on offsprings and loss or death of the biological assets as of December 31, 2020 and furnish the City Accountant of the same to serve as the basis for any adjustment of the Breeding Stocks account.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to remit the unexpended balances of Due to NGAs account to the respective Source Agencies or to the National Treasury as additional resources to be used for other developmental projects of the national government.

We recommended that Management require the CSWD Officer, in cooperation with the City Accountant and City Treasurer, to submit copy of SAC Forms and photo of beneficiary during actual receipt of financial assistance for further evaluation of the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management require the City Accountant and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to submit the lacking documents to support the payments made to the various suppliers and ensure that all disbursement vouchers are properly and duly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the City Accountant to submit the lacking documents  to the Audit Team to support the payments made to the contractor and to ensure that all disbursement vouchers are properly and duly supported with complete documentation;
Instruct the City Accountant to deduct the  retention fee from the payment to the contractor or require warranty security in lieu of retention money  in order to assure that any defects discovered within the warranty period be corrected; and
Direct the BAC to ensure that winning bidders are required to post performance security prior to the signing of contract to guarantee the faithful performance of its obligations to the City Government.

We recommended that Management instruct the BAC Chairman to require the contractor to submit CSHP duly approved by the DOLE to ensure protection and welfare of the workers at the construction site and in compliance with Occupational Safety and Health Standards set by DOLE.

We recommended that Management instruct the City Accountant to strictly adhere to the existing laws, rules and regulations on the proper application of cash advances and ensure that payments shall be made by check directly payable to the creditor in order that applicable withholding taxes can be deducted and remitted to the BIR.
Also, we recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to require Mayor’s Permit of the Supplier before effecting payment of its claim.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the City Assessor to prioritize the registration and transfer of title of the land in the  name of the City government to ensure ownership and to protect the interest of the City Government; and
Require the City Accountant to submit the lacking documents to support the payment made and ensure that all disbursement vouchers are properly and duly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management direct the GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team to ensure that GAD programs and projects implemented by the City Government be monitored properly and evaluated in order to determine the effectiveness thereof by requiring them to submit GAD evaluation report on the accomplishments (outcome level) on the programs and projects.   Further, we recommended that the GAD Focal Person coordinate with BNS, RHU Personnel and CSWD Officer to monitor and get feedback from the end-users through survey or interview in order to assess the ultimate needs of the individuals to avoid needless spending of GAD funds.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to immediately transfer all the unexpended/unobligated balances of the 2020 QRF and MOOE - MF to the Trust Fund books for easy monitoring of the utilization and to ensure the availability of funds to support programs and activities under the LDRRMF.
We further recommended that Management require the City Accountant to prepare the necessary journal entries to transfer the funds and the City Treasurer to prepare the check for the transfer of the funds in order to facilitate the reversion of the unutilized balance of 2015 DRMMF in the Trust Fund to the General Fund.

We recommended that Management:
Reorganize the City Local Solid Waste Management Board to include the Chairperson of the SK Federation in the formulation and implementation of the solid waste management plans and programs. Also, to acknowledge the role of youth as one of the key influencers in environment advocacy; and
Instruct the Focal Person on Solid Waste Management to require all the barangays to maintain MRFs as mandated under RA No. 9003 to ensure proper disposal of solid waste towards protection of public health and its environment.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to employ the use of ATM payroll system for the payment of wages to JO workers, monetary benefits of officials and employees, and salaries and incentives of SP members to deter possible risks of loss of government funds through robbery or other similar incidents or misapplication.
We also recommended that Management instruct the City Human Resource Management Officer (CHRMO) to request the LBP Manager for the enrolment of the 296 JO workers to ATM payroll account.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant to analyze and determine the causes pertaining to the incurrence of negative balances in the subsidiary ledgers maintained for the Due to GSIS account, initiate immediate adjustments to correct the balances, and/or require the refund of the remittances more than the amount withheld from the salaries of City officials and employees to protect the interest of the City Government.

We recommended that the City Accountant stop the existing practice of recording unliquidated cash advances that remained outstanding at the end of the year and thereby make necessary adjustments/reversion of the previous entry to correct the balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements as follows:

General Fund

Prior Year’s Adjustments		₱5,402.00

Gangan; and

Send notices to suppliers informing them of their unclaimed checks, which have been cancelled. In the event that the suppliers/claimants are not anymore willing to collect their claims, the City may revert the checks subject for replacement to the unappropriated surplus.

General Fund

We recommended that the City Accountant:
Regularly check, monitor and keep the Accounts Payable account updated and adequate;
Evaluate unclaimed obligations totaling ₱2,625,883.79that remained outstanding for two years or more.  In the event that no actual claim, administrative or judicial, has been filed or which is not covered by perfected contracts or record on said payables, he may recommend for the reversion of the same pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445 and 5th Indorsement of former COA Commissioner Celso D. Gangan; and
Send notices to suppliers informing them of their unclaimed checks, which have been cancelled. In the event that the suppliers/claimants are not anymore willing to collect their claims, the City may revert the checks subject for replacement to the unappropriated surplus.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
Ascertain if there are employees from the list of personnel with outstanding cash advances, who are still connected with other government national agencies and immediately send the bill/demand letter to concerned personnel. Make representation on this regard to the Head of the Agency concerned and its Accountant; and
Request for the write-off of long outstanding advances granted to officers and employees which remained non-moving for 10 years or more and where settlement/collectability could no longer be ascertained following the procedures under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the City Engineer include the installation of signboards/ billboards at the project sites of all infrastructure projects regardless of funding source as pay item in the program of works which will then be included in the infrastructure contract.

We recommended that:
The City Accountant ascertain that claims of contractor for work accomplishments on infrastructure projects are supported with Work Contracts executed by and between the City of Scan Carlos and the winning contractor.
On the other hand, in case the transaction involved procurement of equipment, supplies and materials, it must be supported with Purchase Order only.
The City/Project Engineer require the contractor to indicate the exact date of receipt of Notice of Award, post the required performance security and enter into contract with the City Government within 10 days from receipt of NOA in compliance with the requirement of Section 37.2.1, Revised Rules and Regulations of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the City Assessor’s Office fast track the updating of Assessment Rolls in the computer system so that the City is ready to implement the new assessed values of real property based on the CY 2018 General Revision of Real Property Assessment.  Henceforth, the CAO to undertake the general revision of real property assessments every three years pursuant to Section 219 of RANo. 7160.

We recommended that Management require the City officials concerned to submit the necessary documents to support the distribution of food and goods, and the release of construction materials to the City Accountant to serve as basis in the recording of the issuance thereof in the books of the city in order to reflect the correct amount of balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Accountant to effect the adjusting entries to reclassify the completed infrastructure asset to its appropriate asset account and close the Construction in Progress account to reflect the correct balances of the accounts in the financial statements.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management exert more effort to collect the receivables from the debtors to provide funds for other intended priority projects of the City.  Furthermore, we recommended that Management comply with the requirements and procedures provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for dormant receivables which were non-moving for more than ten years and where collections thereof could no longer be ascertained.

We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management allocate the required 1% of IRA for the LCPC as provided under Section 15 of R.A. 9344, re-establish its LCPC and direct its members to prepare the Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for Children; and maximize utilization of the allocated LCPC Fund to the identified PPAs pursuant to DILG MC No. 2012-120.

Impose the Liquidated Damages totaling to ₱6,329,534.60 to the Supplier concerned due to incurrence of delay in the delivery of the items purchased pursuant to Section V of the Procurement Manual for Local Government Units, Volume II – Goods and Services and Section 3.1, Annex “D” of the 2016 Revised IRR of R A 9184.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  instruct the  City Officials concerned to stop charging  expenses not related  to disaster mitigation, prevention, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery and be more prudent in the utilization of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund  taking into consideration the existing rules and regulations governing the utilization of the said fund.

We recommended that Management:

Require the City Budget Officer to allot an amount of each project in the budget identified in the Solid Waste Management Fund before submission to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for review and approval pursuant to the aforementioned regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Planning and Development Coordinator to ensure that all projects, programs and activities (PPAs) charged against the development fund are within the allowable expenses enlisted in Sections 2.0 and 3.0 of the same Circular and prioritize those PPAs that are more beneficial to the LGU’s constituents in the long term.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Stop granting honoraria to the LGU officials and employees including officials from the National Government Agencies who are not specifically included under Section 5.0 of CHED Memorandum Order No. 42 Series of 2016.  Also, require them to refund the amount of Ᵽ132,000.00 considering that they are not among the members of the Board of Trustees of the Binalatongan Community College pursuant to the aforementioned CHED Memorandum Order;
Require the ALCU representative and the Vice-Chairman of the Board to refund the amount of Ᵽ7,000.00 and Ᵽ4,000.00, respectively, since they were not present during the Board meeting held on April 16, 2019 as certified by the Board Secretary pursuant to Section 5.0 of the DBM NBC Circular No. 2007-510 dated May 8, 2007.

We maintained our stand that the claimants  were not members of the Board of Trustees as enumerated in the aforementioned composition of the mandated governing body; hence not allowed to receive honoraria, thus disallowed in audit.
For the the load allowance received by the College Administrator, we regret to inform the Management that the expense incurred by the official concerned was an irregular expenditure in nature; hence,  should be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management see to it that Gender and Development (GAD) Plan of the agency will fully address and adhere to the basic purpose and objectives towards the role of women in becoming a participative partner in nation building and development with the end view of protecting their lives and rights and to live a modest life in a society.

We recommended that the Members of the City School Board:

Maximize the utilization of SEF to attain its objectives and take appropriate actions such as realignment or reprogramming, if necessary.
