observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accountant ensure that SET Receivables and Deferred SET Income are adjusted every month upon actual collection of RPT income from the taxpayers pursuant to second paragraph of     Section 20, MNGAS for LGUs, Volume 1 to present reliable balances of the affected accounts.

We recommended that:
The City Treasurer furnish the City Accountant with a duly certified list of taxpayers and amount due and collectible at the beginning of the year to support the recording of RPT/SET Receivables in the books pursuant to first paragraph of Section 20, MNGAS for LGUs, Volume 1; and
The City Accountant request from the ITCS the yearly breakdown of RPT/SET Receivables at the end of the year as supporting schedule for the outstanding or uncollected SET Receivable.

We recommended that the Cashier judiciously review the net amount of all payrolls before finalizing the amount of cash advance for salaries and wages to avoid excess/deficient amount of cash advance for payroll.

We recommended that the Disbursing Officer record the refund/return of the unclaimed salaries/wages in the credit column of the cashbook so the balance of her accountability could be easily determined pursuant to Section 28, MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended that:
(a) The City Mayor direct the City Accountant to immediately transfer the unexpended LDRRMF for CYs 2013 to 2017 to the Special Trust Fund and prepare Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for the reversion of the     CYs 2011 and 2012 unutilized LDRRMF to the unappropriated surplus pursuant to Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.13, respectively of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated                 September 12, 2012; and
(b) The Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council update the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund and Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) and maximize the utilization of the prior years’ LDRRMF as well as the current year’s fund so it could serve the purpose of equipping the City against the adverse impact of hazards and related disasters.

We recommended that the City School Board:
Allocate and utilize strictly the SEF in accordance with Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and the provisions of Joint Circular No. 1 series of 2017 of the DepEd, DBM and DILG to attain the efficient utilization of SEF resources;
Justify the use of the SEF for expenses not in accordance with its primary purpose; and
Refrain from charging unauthorized expenses to the SEF budget specifically the construction and improvement of Administrative buildings. If there are still CY 2018 unimplemented projects and activities which are not among the allowable expenses chargeable against the SEF, immediately realign such projects to other priority projects mentioned in the JC.

We recommended that the City School Board:
Allocate and utilize strictly the SEF in accordance with Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and the provisions of Joint Circular No. 1 series of 2017 of the DepEd, DBM and DILG to attain the efficient utilization of SEF resources;
Justify the use of the SEF for expenses not in accordance with its primary purpose; and
Refrain from charging unauthorized expenses to the SEF budget specifically the construction and improvement of Administrative buildings. If there are still CY 2018 unimplemented projects and activities which are not among the allowable expenses chargeable against the SEF, immediately realign such projects to other priority projects mentioned in the JC.

We recommended that the City Government purchase another bulldozer or immediately rehabilitate the old bulldozer if practicable so that wastes dumped at the SLF can be compacted promptly to reduce volume and to ensure uninterrupted operation at the SLF.
We further recommended that the City maintain a well-run SLF which is free from nuisance or vectors and construct a concrete vault for the proper disposal of hospital wastes to ensure protection of public health and environment pursuant to RA No. 9003.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to review the composition of the Due to LGUs account under the TF and reclassify the fund transfer from various NGAs and GOCCs as Due to National Government Agencies or Due to GOCCs instead of Due to LGUs to conform with the Revised Chart of Accounts.

We recommended that the Human Resource and Management Office update and closely monitor balances of vacation/service credits and properly review application for monetization prior to approval to ensure compliance with the requirements under Section 1, Rule IV of CSC and DBM Joint Circular No. 2-97 dated June 25, 1997.
Further, we recommended that employees with negative vacation/service credit balances after monetization must return the money value of the monetized vacation leave.
For purposes of computing the leave credits to be qualified for monetization pursuant to Section 23 of the Omnibus Rules on Leaves & EO     No. 40, s. 2001, the OHRM take the total vacation and sick leave credits as the available credits for monetization provided that the maximum number of days per application shall be ten days and the maximum number of days to be monetized in a calendar year shall be 30 days.

We recommended that the CWD send immediately demand letters to the concerned government and private consumers who have overdue water accounts pursuant to Section 15 of Omnibus Ordinance regulating the operation of the City Water Works No. 07-66. If the consumers still fail to pay, endorse to the City Legal Office thru the City Mayor’s Office the overdue accounts for legal action and effect water disconnection without further delay.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
Prepare necessary adjusting journal entry to correct  understatement of year-end overdue water bills by debiting Accounts Receivables and crediting Business Income from Waterworks fees; and
Recognize receivable and business income for all remaining unpaid/past due water bill accounts of government agencies and private consumers (inclusive of 25% surcharge and 2% interest) in the CWD books as soon as the schedules/listings duly signed by the Department Head are submitted for recording, pursuant to PPSAS 9 to reflect reliable balances of the affected accounts as well as reveal the actual operations/performance of the City Waterworks Department.

We recommended that:
The City Accountant require the concerned NGOs/POs to submit the final Fund Utilization Reports on the financial assistance granted based on the period specified in the MOA or within 60 days after the completion of the project or activity pursuant to item 5.4 of COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 25, 2007; and
The City Mayor ensure that the MOA for fund transfer to NGOs/POs include provision/obligation of the recipient NGOs/POs to submit liquidation reports within a specified period or after the purpose of the fund transfer has been served pursuant to COA Circular No. 2007-001.

We recommended that the City Treasurer:
Strictly monitor payment of Real Property/Special Education Tax by the taxpayers and send immediately demand letters to those who failed to pay their taxes due on time.  Further, remind taxpayers that unpaid amount shall be subjected to 2% interest per month until 36 months pursuant to the provisions of Section 255 of RA No. 7160;
Consider best solutions to collect past due RPT/SET receivables including interest and intensify collection of prior years’ taxes with the assistance of the CTO- Land Tax Division; and
Secure copies of the RPT/SET Receivables from Information Technology & Computer Services (ITCS) office which could be used as a tool for monitoring of past due accounts.

We recommended that:
The CLDO conduct a monthly monitoring of the loans granted to various cooperatives and livelihood associations, send reminder letters, in case of delay, and improve collection effort so that other cooperatives could also avail loans for livelihood projects; and
The City Accountant exert diligent effort to determine the recipients of the Cattle and Swine Dispersal Program, establish the existence and validity of the loan receivable account and initiate loan collection. If found no longer collectible, inform the CLDO to prepare a request for authority to write-off the account pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the CSWD conduct regular monitoring of the loans granted to various livelihood associations, send reminder letters to those who are not compliant to the agreement, develop strategies to improve the program and evaluate the status of the implementation of the project to determine whether the objectives were attained.

We recommended that:
The City Accountant prepare a journal entry voucher (JEV) to derecognize the demolished building and the corresponding contra-asset account; and
The City Engineer turn-over the salvaged/re-usable materials of the demolished Local Civil Registry Building to the General Services Office.

We recommended that:
The City Accountant immediately seek the assistance of Philhealth regarding the unremitted Philhealth contributions and request for the condonation of interests and surcharges, if possible. If the request is not granted by Philhealth, the personnel in the Accounting Office responsible for the non-remittance of withheld premiums has to assume the interest and surcharges; and
The personnel in charge at the City Accounting Office ensure that remittance of Philhealth contributions follows the correct payment schedule prescribed under Philhealth Circular No. 1, series of 2014.

We recommended that:
The City Accountant identify employees with unremitted premiums or loan amortization and remit the prior years’ unremitted premiums and loans to the concerned corporations without further delay; and
The personnel in charge at the City Accounting Office ensure that all withheld premiums and loan amortizations are remitted based on the prescribed payment schedule of the two corporations.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accountant and General
Services Officer to reconcile their records, after which make the necessary
adjustments thereof.

We recommended the City Accountant to:
a. Compute the depreciation cost of each property, plant and equipment;
and
b. Prepare the necessary journal entry to take up depreciation expense
for the current year and accumulated depreciation for prior years.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management cause
the immediate transfer of ownership and registration of the land purchased in the
name of the City to address the risk of possible court litigation and to establish the
proof of ownership.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended Management the following
courses of action:
1. Create an Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of public
infrastructures especially the local road networks;
2. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of local roads
under the jurisdiction and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of
the Local Road Network at the end of the accounting period;
3. Direct the City Accountant to maintain Local Road Network Ledger
Card and keep separate subsidiary ledgers for each individual road
network components;
4. Inform the General Services Officer or Property Officer to periodically
render Report on Local Road Networks, and furnish copies to the
concerned offices. Also, the City Engineer’s Office should provide the
City Accountant and the General Services Officer or Property Officer
with the complete description and cost segregation of road components
for road projects; and
5. Ensure coordination among LGU Offices to properly implement the
accounting and reporting guidelines of COA Circular No. 2015-008
dated November 5, 2015.

We reiterated the previous year’s recommendation that Management
instruct the City Accountant to cause strictly the immediate liquidation of all
outstanding cash advances by issuing final demand letters direct to the persons
concerned and if no liquidations have been made, impose the sanction of
withholding the salaries of those who fail to settle their accounts after due notice.

We further recommended that Management require the City Accountant
and Acting City Treasurer to monitor strictly the settlement of unliquidated cash
advances and stop the practice of granting additional cash advances to City
Officials and employees until prior advances have been liquidated. Subsequently,
officials and employees with outstanding cash advances who resigned or retired
from the service should be required to settle/liquidate their cash advances before
issuing clearance from money and property accountability.

We recommended that Management enforce collection of the outstanding
receivables from the debtors to provide funds for other intended priority projects of
the City. We also recommended that Management comply with the guidelines
under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for dormant
receivables which were non-moving for more than ten years and where collections
thereof could no longer be ascertained.

We recommended Management to comply Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445 on the
reversion of Accounts Payables which have been outstanding for two years or more
and to examine and review the nature of the disbursement vouchers and its
supporting documents of the outstanding accounts and communicate with the
payees for possible payment of the claims if the transactions are found valid and
regular.

We recommended that management require the correction of the noted
deficiencies for proper implementation of the infrastructure projects of the City to
prevent the incurrence of such deficiencies in the future and submit the required
supporting documents to complete the technical evaluation of the projects
inspected.

We recommended management to fully utilize its 20% Development Fund
by ensuring that all planned projects, programs and activities under the said fund
be implemented in order to achieve the desired benefit intended for its
beneficiaries.

Also, we recommended the Local Chief Executive to direct the BAC
Chairman and City Planning & Development Officer to properly plan and schedule
all procurement activities pertaining to development projects to pave way for
timely implementation and completion thereof.

We reiterated the previous year’s recommendation that Management
enforce the Ecological Solid Waste Management Program particularly establishing
sanitary landfill by appropriating funds which shall be utilized to attain the
objective set forth in Republic Act 9003 and cause the approval of the Solid Waste
Management Plan (SWMP) for concrete guide in the implementation thereof.

We recommended Management to direct the City Disaster Risk Reduction
and Management Council thru the CDRRM Officer to formulate and implement a
comprehensive and integrated LDRRMP and carry out the programs, projects and
activities identified therein to utilize fully the LDRRMF.

We recommended management to ensure the effective and efficient delivery
of benefits to the constituents and to:
a) Incorporate projects, programs and activities in the Annual GAD Plan
and Budget that are responsive to the gender issues of the local
government constituents;
b) Fully utilize the Annual GAD Fund in accordance with the enacted and
DILG-approved GPB; and
c) Strictly monitor the implementation of the projects, programs and
activities.

Furthermore, we recommended that Management direct the Local GAD
Focal Point Team to establish the GAD Database and to provide necessary
resources/equipment to facilitate the creation of the sex-disaggregated database
which shall serve as a basis for a gender-responsive planning, program and policy
formulation.

We recommended Management to:
a) Allocate at least 1% of IRA for the Local Council for the Protection of
Children Fund;
b) Direct members of the LCPC to prepare Local Development Plan for
Children (LDPC) and Local Investment Plan for Children (LIPC), and
c) Maximize utilization of the allocated LCPC Fund to the identified
Programs, Projects and Activities.

We recommended that the City Treasury personnel continue its intensified
tax collection campaign to maximize collection of real property taxes and other
taxes due the City. We have further recommended that City avail the remedies by
administrative action through levy on real property or by judicial actions provided
under the Local Government of 1991.

We recommended that Management continue to collect and remit taxes due
the national government on time. Likewise, the City Accounting Office is advised
to be vigilant so as not to incur under/over-remittance of taxes withheld.

We recommended that management immediately enforce the settlement of
all audit disallowances in compliance with Sections 5.4 and 7.1.1, Chapter II of
COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Likewise, we enjoined
management to be more vigilant in the implementation of the existing accounting
and auditing rules and regulations in the processing of claims to avoid audit
suspensions and disallowances.

observations.recommendation:
The City Mayor submit documents or proof that the City has generated monetary savings as a result of the suggestions, inventions, superior accomplishments and other personal efforts of the employees or group of employees as basis for the grant of monetary awards using the SACA PRAISE as authority.

The San Carlos City PRAISE Committee submit evaluation reports or any documentation recognizing the productivity, creativeness made or ethical behavior of employees or group of employees making them eligible for the PRAISE monetary awards and incentives.

The City Accountant ensure that the amount of incentives to be granted shall not exceed 20% of the monetary savings generated from inventions, suggestions and superior accomplishments pursuant to the policies of the CSC approved San Carlos City PRAISE.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The City Accountant and the City General Service Officer (CGSO) implement the immediate reconciliation of their records on PPE to fully eliminate discrepancies. Moreover, the aforesaid two offices conduct regular reconciliation of their records to reflect reliable balances of the affected PPE accounts.
The CGSO dropped from its inventory report and correspondingly adjust the Property Cards of Motor Vehicles that were already transferred to the barangays.
The CGSO provide the Accounting Office with appropriate documents for unrecorded properties costing ₱6,850,000.00 that will serve as basis by the Accounting personnel for recording in the books.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The GSO submit the IIRUP to the Appraisal Committee so they could immediately conduct appraisal, determine the appraised value and prepare a report thereon.
After the appraised values are determined,  the GSO immediately file the application for disposal of the unserviceable vehicles, thru the City Mayor, with an IIRUP to the Office of the Audit Team Leader for inspection and determination whether the subject property is with value or without value pursuant to Article 444 of the Rules and Regulations Implementing the Local Government Code of 1991.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The City Accountant record in the books the two unbooked donated Motor Vehicles based on available GSO records and prepare necessary adjustment for the cost of the donated ambulance from PCSO.
The GSO refer to the City Appraisal Committee the six properties without appraised or fair market values so that they could immediately conduct appraisal valuation of said motor vehicles.
The Appraisal Committee submit to the Accounting Office, thru the GSO, the result of the appraisal so that the City Accountant has a concrete basis in recording the donated properties in the books.
In order to facilitate recording of all subsequent properties acquired thru donation, close coordination between the GSO Head and the City Accountant is encouraged.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The CGSO facilitate the processing of the TCT of lots owned by the City which are not covered with TCTs and ensure that all land owned by the City are properly covered with titles to support its ownership.
The City Accountant spearhead and fast track the reconciliation of records on land owned by the City by coordinating with the GSO and the City Assessor’s Office in order to completely account the City’s landholdings.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Treasurer stop the practice of paying procurement through cash advance and adhere to the general principle that all payment must be made by check and payable directly to the supplier or contractor except in certain circumstances enumerated in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
The BAC shall recommend award only to supplier whose quotation has no condition as to the mode of payment.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The City Government ascertain that transfer of funds/financial assistance must be released only to NGOs/POs which meet the requisites for entitlement to government funds pursuant to 4.4 of COA Circular No. 2007-001 dated October 25, 2007.
The City official, responsible for the review of the project proposal or application for funding by the NGO/PO, must ensure that the latter has the capacity to undertake the project, has previously implemented similar project and the purpose of the establishment of NGO corresponds to the objective of the financial assistance.
The City Accountant monitor the liquidation report of SAVER, Inc.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant prepare immediately a list of unexpended PDAF balances which will serve as the basis in the preparation of disbursement vouchers for the refund of the amount to the Bureau of Treasury in compliance with COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 9-81, COA Circular No. 94-013, Executive Order No. 431 and the Supreme Court ruling issued on                November 19, 2013.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The Cashier records only cash advances received and payments/refunds/adjustments against the cash advances granted to her in her cashbook pursuant to Section 28 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II.
The City Treasurer discontinue the practice of transferring cash advance from one accountable officer to another officer and strictly adhere to the provision of Section 75 of PD No. 1445.
Moreover, she is advised to record payments/disbursements out of her cash advance by fund so the balance of her accountability by fund could be easily determined.  Also, she must properly fill out the balance column of the cashbook for easy reconciliation of the cashbook balance with the subsidiary ledger balance.
The Cashier/Disbursing Officer issue a certification as to the correctness and completeness of the recorded cash transactions at the end of the month after the last entry in the cash book pursuant to Section 28 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Liquidation Report for the cash advance intended for the supplemental feeding program must be supported with acceptable proof of receipt of payment for disbursements charged to the cash advance pursuant to   COA Circular No. 2004-006 dated September 9, 2004.  All petty or small expenses costing ₱300.00 or less shall likewise be supported with Certification of Expenses not Requiring Receipts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2017-009 dated June 19, 2017.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant require the DOs to strictly comply with the documentary requirements provided under Item 1.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, every time a request for cash advance for payroll is submitted for approval.

Recommendation:
We recommended that SCCH Accounting Office immediately prepare the Abstract of Collections upon receipt of the breakdown of reimbursed claim from Philhealth so that the City Accounting Department could correctly record the hospital revenues on the month they pertain.
In case of delayed receipt of reimbursed claim from Philhealth, the City Accounting Department must record a receivable from Philhealth in order to recognize the revenues in the same period the hospital reported the expenses that brought them.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer send immediately confirmation letter to the bank requesting for reference document to establish the validity of said deposit.
In the event that the amount could not anymore be identified by the bank, the City Accountant recommends that it shall be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund considering that it has remained outstanding for more than eight years and against which no actual claim has been filed pursuant to Section 96 of P.D. 1445.
Henceforth, the City Accounting Office and the City Treasurer’s Office must immediately confirm with the bank any unidentified deposits that will be credited to the City’s bank accounts.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The City School Board allocate and utilize strictly the SEF in accordance with Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and the provisions of JC No. 1 series of 2017 of the DepEd, DBM and DILG to attain the efficient utilization of SEF resources.
The CLSB justify the use of the SEF for expenses not in accordance with its primary purpose and shall refrain from charging unauthorized expenses to the SEF budget.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the LCPC to prepare the Work and Financial Plan of the LCPC as well as LDPC and LIPC so that all priority PPAs for children are allocated with appropriate funding and maximize the utilization of LCPC fund to identified PPAs enumerated in DILG Memorandum Circular 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 to ensure that rights and welfare of children are well protected.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accounting Office and all the offices involved in the project implementation regularly reconcile their records regarding completed and on-going projects.  Moreover, the Accounting Office must transfer all completed projects after the reconciliation process or before the year ends so that the affected accounts are accordingly adjusted and in order to have a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accounting and the City Engineering Offices reconcile their records regarding completed projects before the year ends so that all completed projects are timely transferred to the proper PPE account classification to avoid overstatement of the CIP account as at year end.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor and the City Development Council (CDC) adhere to the provisions of the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01 dated February 22, 2017 with regard to the utilization of the 20% Development Fund and must use the same for the development projects or program that partake the nature of investments or capital expenditures that contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the City.
Further, we recommended that the City Officials concerned justify the charging of the Construction of ABC Hall Phase IV against the 20% Development Fund.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City refrain from using the open space inside the sanitary landfill as disposal area for sharps and infectious wastes and adopt a method for the proper disposal of aforesaid wastes to protect the people and environment from potential hazard to human health or environment.

Recommendation:
We recommended that the City Officials involved in the BuB planning process ensure that priority projects to be included in the BuB Menu of Programs are properly evaluated and adequately planned to avoid revisions in the Program of Work  in order to fast track project implementation.

We reiterated past years’ recommendation to the management through the
City Accountant to demand for the immediate liquidation of all outstanding cash
advances by issuing final demand letters direct to the persons concerned and if the
liquidation is not effected, impose the sanction of withholding the salaries of those
who still fail to settle their accounts after issuing the notice. Also, institute
appropriate legal measures/ actions to compel all concerned officials and employees
to settle their accounts especially those with huge account balances.

We recommended that management cause the immediate transfer of
ownership and registration of the lands purchased in the name of the city to address
the risk of possible court litigation and to establish the proof of ownership.

We have reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations that the
Local Chief Executive (a) create inventory committee to facilitate the completion
of the inventory taking for all their properties and the preparation and submission
of the corresponding report thereof, (b) require the City Accountant and their
CGSO to maintain and update their respective property records and regularly
reconcile them, (c) create disposal committee to take the appropriate action for the
disposition of the unserviceable properties.

We recommended that management exert enough effort to collect the
amount loaned to borrowers/cooperatives in order for the city to use the funds for
other intended priority projects.

We recommended that management:
1. Require the posting to PhilGEPs the results of bidding in accordance
with Section 37.4.2 of the Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 (R.A.
9184).
2. Require the correction of the noted deficiencies and the proper
implementation of the infrastructure projects of the City to prevent the
incurrence of such deficiencies in the future.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive to cause the immediate
reversion of the unexpended balance of the PDAF covered by the permanent
injunction of the Supreme Court to the Bureau of Treasury (BTr) in compliance to
the Supreme Court En Banc Decision dated November 19, 2013. Likewise, stop to
utilize the fund as mandated therein.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive together with other City
Officials to appropriate funds for the City’s Ecological Solid Waste Management
Program as provided under Republic Act 9003 in order to ensure the protection of
public health and environment. In addition, cause the approval of its Solid Waste
Management Plan (SWMP) for concrete guide in the implementation thereof.

We recommended that the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
Officer comply strictly with Section 12.c.24 of R.A. 10121 and Section 5.1.5 of
COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 on the submission
requirement of the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF to the
Office of the Auditor. Failure to do so, the said officer shall be dealt with
administratively under Section 122 of PD 1445.

We recommended that management maximize the utilization of GAD funds
on its identified PPAs and implement them as prioritized. It is also advised that the
City Accountant must not charge the non-related obligations to the said fund as the
different offices have their own budget.

We recommended that the City Treasury personnel to continue its intensified
tax collection campaign to maximize collection of real property taxes and other
taxes due to the city government in order to increase the delivery of basic services.
We have further recommended that identified delinquent real property taxpayers
avail the remedies by administrative action through levy on real property or by
judicial actions provided under the Local Government of 1991.

We recommended that management expedite the sale of the remaining units
and collection of unpaid housing units to augment the income of the city to be used
for its other beneficial projects.

We recommended that management should continue to collect and remit
taxes due the national government on time. Likewise, the City Accounting Office is
advised to be vigilant so as not to incur under/over-remittance of taxes withheld.

We recommended that management immediately enforce the settlement of all
audit suspensions/disallowances in compliance with Section 5.4 and 7.1.1, Chapter
II of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009. Likewise, we enjoin
management to be more vigilant in the implementation of the existing accounting
and auditing rules and regulations in the processing of claims to avoid audit
suspensions and disallowances.

The City Accountant and the City Engineer’s Office regularly reconcile their records regarding completed and on-going projects so that the affected accounts are accordingly adjusted and in order to have a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

It is also suggested that all departments of the City involve in the project implementation must submit their monthly project status report for consolidation and monitoring. 

The City Mayor create monitoring team who will take charge in the consolidation of reports of completed and on-going projects, programs and activities of all implementing offices of the City.

observations.recommendation:
For the proper management and handling of the PPE, we have recommended the following:
Conduct the required annual physical count of the PPE as of December of every year. The actual count may be undertaken any month during the current year, but has to be updated at year-end. This procedure is a must in order to know the presence/physical existence of the fixed assets and their conditions; and to identify, segregate and reclassify unserviceable assets. Reconciliation of the result of the count with the accounting records is to be undertaken to effect the necessary adjustments; and to pinpoint responsibility/accountability/liability for missing PPEs.
Maintain/update the subsidiary ledgers, PPE ledger cards, PARs and placing of property tags which are required under MNGAS for proper control and monitoring of the PPEs and for facility in identifying them.
For control and monitoring make use of Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) for semi-expendable items to avoid loss, as these are usually small items.

We  recommended that management:
1.  Require the posting to PhilGEPs the results of bidding in accordance with Section 37.4.2 of the Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 (R.A. 9184).
2. Require the correction of the noted deficiencies and the proper implementation of the infrastructure projects of the City to prevent the incurrence of such deficiencies in the future.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive implement the programs/projects/activities funded from the National Government Agencies (NGAs) so that the purposes for which these funds were granted could be  achieved,  thus benefitting the constituents of the City.

We recommended that management:
Require the City Planning and Development Officer to adhere strictly with the provisions of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 of the DILG and DBM, the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% of the Annual Internal Allotment (IRA) for programming of development projects, specifically Sections 3 and 4 thereof;  and
Implement the identified projects and that a change thereto should be substantially addressed in the succeeding Annual Investment Plan.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive maximize the utilization of the funds appropriated for the Ecological Solid Waste Management Program as provided under Republic Act 9003 and cause the approval of the Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) for concrete guide in the implementation thereof.

We recommended that management comply with the aforementioned provisions of law and regulations on the creation of CDRRMO and its personnel, respectively and require the City Accountant to comply with the preparation and submission of the monthly sources and utilization of the LDRRMF in compliance with Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and RA 10121 in order to determine the capacity of the city to sustain the delivery of services in times of calamities.

We recommended that management maximize the utilization of GAD funds on its identified PPAs and implement them as prioritized. It is also advised that the City Accountant must not charge the non-related obligations to the said fund as the different offices have their own budget.
Likewise, the GAD Focal Person review the GAD plan and budget vis-a-vis GAD Accomplishment Report to determine programs that truly promote women empowerment and gender equality. They should be guided by the desired outcomes and goals embodied in the MCW, other relevant laws, PPGD, WEDGE Plan, succeeding term plans for GAS as well as the UN CEDAW, and other relevant international commitments.

We recommended that the City Treasury personnel intensify tax collection campaign to maximize collection of real property taxes and other taxes due to the city government in order to increase the delivery of basic services and facilities to implement various programs of the Local School Board. We have further recommended that the identified delinquent real property taxpayers avail of the remedies by administrative action through levy on real property or by judicial actions provided under the Local Government of 1991.

We recommended that management require the Market Committee to institute measures/actions to reduce/avoid the delinquencies on market stalls. Likewise, we recommended that they formulate plans/marketing strategy and adopt policies that will enable market stallholders settle their delinquencies on market stall fees relative to the occupancy of vacant/closed stalls in the public market to augment income of the city.

We  recommended that management continue to collect and remit taxes due the national government on time. Likewise, the City Accounting Office should be advised to be vigilant so as not to incur under/over-remittance of taxes withheld.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive should cause the immediate settlement of the balance of unsettled suspensions and disallowances of P1,227,893.63 in compliance with Section 5.4 and 7.1.1, Chapter II of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

It is also suggested that all departments of the City involve in the project implementation must submit their monthly project status report for consolidation and monitoring.

The City submit specific legal basis for the grant of Achievement Incentives in the event that it could not submit aforementioned requirements. Otherwise, we recommended that the said incentives be refunded by the City government officials and employees for lack of specific legal authority.
The incentives given to the barangay officials be refunded for lack of legal basis and besides they are not employees of the City Government.
